title: French onion soup
subtitle: Deep brown, richly savoured, with a gruyère croûte
excerpt: Slowly caramelised onions in a dark beef broth, ladled over a bread croûte buried under melted Gruyère. A classic of the French bistro, built almost entirely from patience.
sourceNotes:
Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), English translation. Soupe à l'oignon method and broth technique.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845) ,  Project Gutenberg. Brown onion soup with bread.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the soup base without the bread or cheese. Cool completely and freeze for up to three months. Make the croûtes fresh when serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The soup base improves over two days in the fridge. Rewarm and grill the croûtes just before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: To cook onions or other sugary food slowly over heat until the natural sugars break down and turn dark brown, producing a deep sweet flavour.
To add liquid (wine, stock, or water) to a hot pan to dissolve the browned bits stuck to the base, incorporating their flavour into the sauce or soup.
A thick slice of bread, often toasted or baked, that forms the base for a grilled cheese topping in soups such as this one.

content.text: French onion soup asks for patience above everything else. The onions need a full hour in the pot before they are ready: collapsing, darkening, then turning a deep mahogany brown that sweetens and concentrates into something almost meaty. Rush that stage and you have onion soup; wait for it and you have something entirely different.
The
croûte
goes on last, under the grill: a thick slice of toast holding a thick layer of Gruyère that melts down into the broth at the edges. Eat it immediately, before the bread softens completely.
What you need
Use a wide, heavy-based pan so the onions spread out and colour evenly. Ovenproof soup bowls make the grilling easier, but any deep bowls will work.
Method
Caramelise the onions
Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large, heavy-based pan over a medium heat. Add {{onion}} onions and a large pinch of salt. Stir to coat in the butter.
Cook over a medium-low heat, stirring every 5 to 8 minutes, for 50 to 60 minutes. The onions will first soften and give off liquid (do not rush this stage with higher heat), then slowly
caramelise
to a deep golden brown. The base of the pan will colour and develop dark patches: that is fine and is part of the flavour.
Add the {{garlic}} of sliced garlic, {{thyme}} of thyme, and {{bay-leaves}} of bay. Cook for 2 more minutes.
Add the stock and simmer
Pour in {{stock-beef}} of beef stock. Scrape the base of the pan with a spoon to
deglaze
all the dark, sticky bits. Bring to a gentle simmer.
Simmer uncovered for 15 minutes. Fish out the thyme stalks and bay leaves. Taste and adjust salt.
Make the croûtes and serve
Heat the grill to its highest setting. Arrange the baguette slices on a baking sheet and toast under the grill for 2 minutes per side until golden.

Ladle the hot soup into ovenproof bowls. Float 2 bread slices on top of each bowl, then pile {{gruyere}} of grated Gruyère generously over the bread. The cheese should cover the bread and hang over the edges.
Place the bowls under the grill for 3 to 4 minutes until the cheese is bubbling and beginning to brown in patches. Serve immediately, warning the bowls are very hot.

items.symptom: Onions are not dark enough after an hour
items.cause: Heat was too low, or the pan was too crowded and the onions steamed rather than fried.
items.fix: Increase the heat slightly and cook uncovered, stirring more frequently. The base of the pan should catch and colour: that is normal and wanted. Scrape and stir it in.

items.cause: The onions are very sweet varieties, or were caramelised more than needed.
items.fix: Add a small splash of red wine vinegar or a teaspoon of Worcestershire sauce to the finished broth. Both cut through the sweetness.

items.symptom: Cheese slides off the bread into the soup
items.cause: The bread was not toasted firmly enough first, or the cheese was added too thickly on one side.
items.fix: Toast the bread until properly crisp before grilling with the cheese. Pile the cheese in the centre and press it lightly so it adheres.

items.cause: Standard bowls crack under the grill heat.
items.fix: Use deep ceramic bowls labelled oven-safe, or assemble the croûtes on a separate baking tray and slide them onto the soup just before serving.

content.text: Where this dish lives

French onion soup is the food of Les Halles, the old Paris market district, where it was eaten by porters in the small hours after a night's work. The recipe in Escoffier is spare: onions, broth, bread, cheese. Nothing has needed adding since. It appears in every French bistro in the world because it is one of those dishes that delivers something a kitchen can't fake: the long labour of a slowly caramelised pan is visible in every spoonful.
